The Casimir effect demonstrates that even empty space contains vacuum fluctuations, producing an attractive force between two neutral, parallel plates placed a few nanometers apart.

facts: The first computer virus, called **Creeper**, was written in 1971 for the TENEX operating system and was designed to move between mainframes, printing the message “I’m the creeper, catch me if you can.”.
The **World Wide Web** was invented by Tim Berners‑Lee in 1989 while he was a researcher at CERN; the first website went live on August 6, 1990, and it remains online today.
contained a hard‑disk‑drive‑style controller for its memory, a technology that was later adapted for flash memory control in modern smartphones.

The **Hubble Space Telescope** was built using a 2.4‑meter primary mirror, but its actual optical system contains 36 hexagonal mirror segments that are individually adjustable to correct for distortion.
The first commercially available GPS receiver was sold in 1988, but it could only provide location data for the U.S. military because international GPS signals were not yet available.

Bitcoin’s original white paper was published under the pseudonym **Satoshi Nakamoto** in 2008, but the true identity of Nakamoto remains unknown and is the subject of ongoing debate among researchers.

The **World’s first programmable robot**, called the “Unimation” industrial robot, was created by George Devol in 1954; it was later commercialized by George Devol’s company Unimation, which became a major supplier of industrial robots in the 1960s.

Neutron stars are so dense that a sugar‑cube‑size amount of their material would weigh about a billion tons on Earth. The observable universe contains more than 100 billion galaxies, each with billions of stars, yet it accounts for only about 5 % of the total mass‑energy; the rest is dark matter and dark energy. In the vacuum of space, water can exist as a solid, liquid, and gas simultaneously at the same temperature and pressure—this is called the triple point, and it occurs at about 0.01 °C and 6.1 mPa. The largest known structure in the universe is the Hercules‑Corona Borealis Great Wall, a galaxy filament spanning roughly 10 billion light‑years, far larger than any individual galaxy cluster. Saturn’s moon Enceladus ejects jets of water vapor and ice particles from its south pole, contributing material that forms part of Saturn’s E‑ring. The Milky Way and the Andromeda Galaxy are on a collision course and are expected to merge in about 4.5 billion years, forming a single elliptical galaxy sometimes nicknamed “Milkomeda.”. Space is not completely empty; a single cubic meter of interstellar space near the Sun contains, on average, about one hydrogen atom.

The microwave oven was discovered accidentally when engineer Percy Spencer noticed a chocolate bar melted in his pocket while he was testing a magnetron for radar technology in 1945.

entries.title: Most people don't know this... The world’s most expensive coffee, Kopi Luwak, is made from ...
facts: The world’s most expensive coffee, Kopi Luwak, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by the Asian palm civet, and the fermentation process in the animal’s digestive tract gives the coffee its distinctive smooth flavor.

Honey never spoils; archaeologists have found pots of honey in ancient Egyptian tombs that are still edible after more than 3,000 years because honey’s low water content and natural acidity inhibit bacterial growth.
The “sweet spot” for the ripeness of a pineapple is when its leaves in the crown can be easily pulled out—this indicates the fruit has reached peak sugar content and is ready to eat.
is lower than its melting point because the high sugar concentration lowers the freezing point, so a frozen watermelon remains partially pliable rather than turning completely solid.

The “umami” taste was only officially recognized as the fifth basic taste in 1908 by Japanese chemist Kikunae Ikeda, who identified glutamate as the compound responsible for the savory flavor in seaweed broth.

The bright orange color of carrots originally came from beta‑carotene, but the first cultivated carrots were purple or white; orange carrots were selectively bred in the 17th century in the Netherlands as a tribute to the House of Orange.

The “crackling” sound when you bite into a fresh apple is caused by the rapid release of water vapor from tiny air‑filled cells that burst under pressure, a phenomenon known as “acoustic cavitation.”.

The spice saffron, derived from the stigmas of Crocus sativus flowers, requires about 150,000 blossoms to produce a single kilogram, making it the most labor‑intensive agricultural product in the world.

entries.script: Most people don't know this... The world’s most expensive coffee, Kopi Luwak, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by the Asian palm civet, and the fermentation process in the animal’s digestive tract gives the coffee its distinctive smooth flavor. Honey never spoils; archaeologists have found pots of honey in ancient Egyptian tombs that are still edible after more than 3,000 years because honey’s low water content and natural acidity inhibit bacterial growth. The “sweet spot” for the ripeness of a pineapple is when its leaves in the crown can be easily pulled out—this indicates the fruit has reached peak sugar content and is ready to eat. is lower than its melting point because the high sugar concentration lowers the freezing point, so a frozen watermelon remains partially pliable rather than turning completely solid. The “umami” taste was only officially recognized as the fifth basic taste in 1908 by Japanese chemist Kikunae Ikeda, who identified glutamate as the compound responsible for the savory flavor in seaweed broth. The bright orange color of carrots originally came from beta‑carotene, but the first cultivated carrots were purple or white; orange carrots were selectively bred in the 17th century in the Netherlands as a tribute to the House of Orange. The “crackling” sound when you bite into a fresh apple is caused by the rapid release of water vapor from tiny air‑filled cells that burst under pressure, a phenomenon known as “acoustic cavitation.”. The spice saffron, derived from the stigmas of Crocus sativus flowers, requires about 150,000 blossoms to produce a single kilogram, making it the most labor‑intensive agricultural product in the world.

entries.title: Mind-blowing fact: In 1518, the city of Strasbourg experienced a “dance fever” ...
facts: In 1518, the city of Strasbourg experienced a “dance fever” in which hundreds of people danced uncontrollably for days, and some reportedly danced themselves to death.
The ancient Roman Empire once appointed a horse, Incitatus, as a senator’s honorary “member of the Senate” to mock the political elite.
During World War II, the United States Army Air Forces trained pigeons to carry secret messages; the most famous pigeon, Cher Ami, delivered crucial intelligence that saved a battalion in France.
In 1835, the United States briefly considered adopting the “St. Helena” calendar, which would have added an extra month called “Sol” to align the year with lunar cycles.
The Great Emu War of 1932 in Australia saw the Australian army deploy soldiers armed with machine guns against a flock of emus, ultimately losing the “battle.”.
In 1910, a meteorite struck a house in the small town of Alvwood, Michigan, becoming the only known instance of a meteorite hitting a residential building.
The first recorded use of a “self‑destruct” message dates to 1702, when a British spy left a coded note that would dissolve in water unless the recipient added vinegar.
In 1974, a Soviet submarine accidentally launched a nuclear torpedo that detonated harmlessly in the Arctic Ocean, a near‑miss event that remained classified until the 1990s.

entries.title: This will blow your mind... The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so big that if placed a...
facts: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so big that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would engulf the orbit of Jupiter.
A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 6 billion tons on Earth.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is currently the most distant human-made object, traveling over 14 billion miles from Earth.
The cosmic microwave background radiation, the afterglow of the Big Bang, has been measured to vary by only about 1 part in 100,000 across the sky.
The faint, diffuse glow of the night sky from Earth’s surface is dominated by starlight reflected off the Moon, not by the Sun.
A black hole’s event horizon can be larger than its singularity; for a 10-solar-mass black hole, the horizon is about 30 kilometers across.
The Hubble Space Telescope requires a maintenance visit every 5–10 years, each servicing mission adding only a few hundred kilograms to the telescope’s mass.
There exists a pulsar, PSR J1748-2446ad, that rotates 716 times per second, making it the fastest known spinning neutron star.

entries.title: Did you know? The Sun’s core temperature is about 15 million °C (27 millio...
facts: The Sun’s core temperature is about 15 million °C (27 million °F), far hotter than the surface meltdowns of the most powerful terrestrial nuclear reactors.
In the Milky Way, there is a 100‑kiloparsec‑wide ring of interstellar dust and gas that is actively forming new stars, even though the galaxy’s “main” disc is only about 30 kpc across.
A neutron star can spin at a rate of over 600 revolutions per second, so it would complete a full rotation in one‑hundredth of a second.

The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, entered interstellar space in 2012 and is now more than 23 billion kilometers from Earth, still traveling at about 17 km/s toward the constellation Ophiuchus.

The largest known volcanic eruption on Earth, the 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora, released ash into the stratosphere that circled the planet and lowered global temperatures by roughly 0.5 °C for several years.
is approaching the Milky Way at about 110 km/s, and the two galaxies are expected to collide in roughly 4 billion years, forming a single elliptical galaxy.

The cosmic microwave background radiation is not perfectly uniform; its temperature varies by only about 1 part in 100,000, yet these tiny variations map the seeds of all large‑scale structure in the universe.

A black hole can gain mass not only from accretion of nearby matter but also from absorbing photons: if a black hole passes through a luminous star‑forming region, it can swallow enough starlight to grow a few percent of its mass over a million years.

entries.title: This will blow your mind... The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, e...
facts: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each capable of forming thousands of connections, called synapses, with other neurons.
The adult human brain consumes about 20% of the body's oxygen and 25% of its glucose, despite accounting for only about 2% of body weight.
The hippocampus, a region critical for forming new memories, continues to generate new neurons throughout life, a process known as adult neurogenesis.
The brain’s white matter is composed largely of myelinated axons, which are insulated by fatty tissue to speed up electrical signal transmission.
Brain activity follows a circadian rhythm, aligning peak cognitive performance with daylight hours and reducing alertness during nighttime.
A single neuron can release many thousands of neurotransmitter molecules at a synapse, yet the overall power required for brain function is only about 20 watts.
The cerebellum, traditionally known for coordinating movement, also contributes to higher-order functions such as language processing and working memory.
The brain’s blood–brain barrier is formed by tightly connected endothelial cells, tightly regulating the passage of substances between the bloodstream and neural tissue.

Neuroplasticity— the brain's ability to reorganize itself— allows it to compensate for injury; for example, motor skills can be relearned by rerouting pathways in adjacent cortical areas.

The "default mode network" is a set of brain regions that becomes active when a person is not focused on the outside world, such as during day‑dreaming or mind‑wandering, and its activity patterns change with age and mental health conditions.
to intense focus (beta waves).

The left and right hemispheres of the brain communicate via the corpus callosum, which contains about 200–300 million axons; this structure can adapt to injuries by strengthening alternative pathways.

The average human brain contains about 10^21 synapses, a number so large that it exceeds the number of atoms in a small grain of sand, illustrating the extraordinary complexity of neural connectivity.

facts: The first computer “bug” was a literal moth; in 1947 engineers recorded a dead moth trapped in a Harvard Mark II relay and taped it with the note “First actual case of bug being found,” coining the term in engineering lore.

The Apollo 11 lunar module’s onboard computer had less processing power than a modern digital wristwatch; it operated at 0.043 MHz with 64 KB of memory, whereas today’s smartwatches run at gigahertz speeds with hundreds of megabytes of RAM.

The QWERTY keyboard layout was originally designed to prevent mechanical typewriters’ typebars from jamming, not for typing efficiency; the staggered arrangement reduces the likelihood of adjacent bars striking each other.
and retains its original text and layout.
In 2013, a single Bitcoin transaction used more electricity than the entire country of Iceland’s annual consumption, highlighting the extreme energy demands of early proof‑of‑work mining.
The “Internet of Things” term was coined in 1999 by Kevin Ashton, a British technology pioneer, during a project to embed RFID tags in supply‑chain items at Procter & Gamble.
The first 1 TB hard drive, introduced by Hitachi in 2007, weighed 550 g and cost roughly US$300,000; today, a 1 TB solid‑state drive can weigh under 10 g and cost less than $50.

NASA’s Voyager 1 spacecraft carries a “Golden Record” containing 115 minutes of Earth sounds and 90 minutes of music, encoded in analog form on a 12‑cm gold‑plated copper disc designed to survive for billions of years in interstellar space.

entries.title: This will blow your mind... The smallest non‑trivial group that is not cyclic is the Kle...
facts: The smallest non‑trivial group that is not cyclic is the Klein four group, consisting of four elements with each non‑identity element having order 2.
as n approaches infinity, and also as an infinite continued fraction with all partial quotients equal to 1.
There are more real numbers than there are rational numbers: the set of real numbers is uncountable, while the set of rational numbers is countable, as proved by Cantor’s diagonal argument.
A prime number can be written as the sum of two squares if and only if it is congruent to 1 modulo 4; for example, 13 = 2² + 3² but 17 = 1² + 4², both conforming to the rule.
The Fibonacci sequence contains every positive integer as a term in some of its subsequences; specifically, for any integer k, there exists a Fibonacci number that is a multiple of k.

The decimal expansion of 1/7 is 0.142857142857…, a repeating cycle of length 6, and the six digits 142857 are cyclic: multiplying the number by 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6 rotates the digits rather than changing the set of digits.
The sum of the reciprocals of the squares converges to \(\pi^2/6\): \(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6\), a result known as the Basel problem, solved by Euler.

A “magic square” of order 3, such as the classic Lo Shu square, exists only for odd orders and has the same magic constant for all rows, columns, and main diagonals; the unique 3×3 Lo Shu magic square sums to 15 in every direction.

entries.title: Wait till you hear this: The ocean contains more than 20,000 species of organisms tha...
facts: The ocean contains more than 20,000 species of organisms that can be found in a single square meter of deep-sea hydrothermal vent ecosystems, a biodiversity density far exceeding that of tropical rainforests.
Roughly 30% of the Earth's surface is covered by ocean, yet only about 5% of its volume has been sampled by modern scientific expeditions, leaving the majority of marine life still unknown.

The Great Pacific Garbage Patch, a floating concentration of plastic waste, is estimated to be twice the size of Germany and contains a density of approximately 1.7 pieces of plastic per square kilometer.
that can grow up to 10 centimeters per day, reaching heights of over 60 meters and covering thousands of square meters of ocean floor.
Seafloor trenches host microorganisms that thrive under pressures exceeding 1,000 atmospheres, such as the deep‑sea bacterium *Methanopyrus kandleri* which can survive temperatures up to 122 °C.
The average salinity of seawater is 35 parts per thousand, but the Salton Sea in California—once a freshwater lake—now averages 44 ppt, making it one of the saltiest bodies of water on Earth.
In 2003, the world’s largest known tsunami, the 2004 Indian Ocean event, released kinetic energy equivalent to 100 times the combined energy of all nuclear weapons in the world’s stockpiles.
Coral reefs can filter up to 1,500 liters of water per larval coral polyps per day, effectively acting as natural seawater purification systems and supporting local fisheries.

facts: The average song length in the 1960s was about 3 minutes and 15 seconds, whereas modern pop singles often average 2 minutes and 50 seconds due to streaming platforms favoring shorter tracks.
and “orchestra” (oke), literally translating to “empty orchestra.”.

In 2009, a single track by the Indian band *Shivaji Raghunath* sold 120,000 copies in its first week, making it the fastest-selling single in India’s history at that time.
The phrase “rock and roll” was originally a euphemism used by the Harlem jazz scene in the 1940s to describe a type of dancing that was considered vulgar.
The first commercially available synthesizer, the RCA Mark II Sound Synthesizer, was built in 1957 and could generate more than 1,000 different timbres.
The 1973 song “Stairway to Heaven” by Led Zeppelin technically contains a hidden twelve-note motif that appears in the guitar solo and the final chord progression.
In 2015, Spotify claimed that 93% of the world’s music consumption came from streaming services, a figure that had grown from 21% in 2007.

entries.title: Wait till you hear this: The first electric light bulb, developed by Joseph Swan and ...
facts: The first electric light bulb, developed by Joseph Swan and Thomas Edison independently, used a carbonized paper filament that was actually 97% cheaper to produce than the tungsten filaments used in modern bulbs.
The original design for the modern-day stainless steel knife was patented by a Scottish watchmaker, James Dewar, in 1903, long before stainless steel was widely used in kitchen utensils.

In 1909, an American engineer named Leo Baekeland invented Bakelite, the first fully synthetic plastic, while trying to create a cheap substitute for shellac; the material later became the cornerstone of the 20th‑century plastic industry.

The first commercially viable insulin production process was established by a Canadian company, Eli Lilly, in 1923, using a surprisingly simple method of mixing human pancreas extracts with a salt solution and filtering it through a cloth.

The iconic “spray paint” aerosol can was invented in 1959 by a German chemist, Günther Schleich, who discovered that a propellant made from compressed air and an explosive gas mixture could deliver paint evenly without clogging.

The invention of the paper clip in 1869 by Johan Vaaler, a Norwegian inventor, was largely forgotten until the 1920s when a Norwegian newspaper featured a photo of a man using a “paper stapler,” inadvertently popularizing the clip worldwide.
The first electronic calculator, the ANITA, was built in 1950 by a team of engineers at the Indian Institute of Science, predating the famous 1960s American calculators by several decades.

The original “toaster” patent filed in 1889 by engineer Albert E. J. Hollingsworth described a two‑in‑one device that could both slice bread and toast it in the same compartment, a concept that was never commercially realized.

entries.title: Did you know? The human eye can distinguish more colors than the average c...
facts: The human eye can distinguish more colors than the average computer screen can display, because our rods and cones can mix signals in ways that RGB dots cannot fully replicate.
Water expands by about 9 % when it freezes, which is why ice floats and can crack pipes in winter.
A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh roughly 6 × 10^14 kilograms on Earth, far exceeding the mass of the entire human population.
The planet Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most planets, meaning its sun rises in the west and sets in the east.
The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so massive that if it were placed at the center of our Solar System, its outer layers would engulf the orbit of Jupiter.
Gold can exist in a liquid state without melting, because its atoms can rearrange into a fluid pattern while remaining solid.
The Earth's magnetic field is generated not by the metallic core alone but by interactions between molten iron and the planet’s rotation, which creates a dynamo effect.

entries.title: Did you know? The “Happy Birthday” song is the most recognisable melody in...
facts: The “Happy Birthday” song is the most recognisable melody in the world, yet it remains under copyright in the United States; the estate of its composers, Mildred J. Hill and Patty S. Hill, collected licensing fees until a 2016 court ruling declared it public domain.

The longest piece of music ever composed is John Cage’s *Organ²/ASLSP* (“As Slow as Possible”), which is being performed in Halberstadt, Germany; the performance began in 2001 and is scheduled to end in the year 2640.

Beethoven continued to compose some of his most celebrated works, including the Ninth Symphony, after becoming completely deaf; he used a special wooden “hearing rod” that transmitted vibrations from the piano to his jaw.

The “Mozart effect,” the claim that listening to Mozart temporarily boosts spatial reasoning, was based on a 1993 study that showed only a 15‑second improvement in a specific test, and subsequent research has failed to replicate any lasting cognitive benefit.

In 2015, a group of researchers discovered that humming a simple tune while walking can improve gait stability and reduce the risk of falls in older adults, likely because the rhythmic vocalization synchronises with the body’s motor patterns.
The world’s first recorded “music video” predates MTV by decades: in 1929, the short film *The Song of the Flame* featured synchronized sound and visual performance of the song “The Blue Danube.”.
refers to Johann Sebastian Bach because the puzzle’s creator, Raymond Smullyan, named several of his puzzles after classical composers as an homage to their intricate structures.

The human brain processes a musical chord change faster than it processes a single spoken word; neuroimaging studies show that the auditory cortex reacts to harmonic progressions within 100 milliseconds, whereas lexical processing typically takes 150–200 milliseconds.
